overview: Existing balanced Top 200 content + Top 200 audience samples
snippet_counts: YouTube (Jagero Podcast), YouTube (Joab Ogato Podcast, Ep. 071)
Radio (Hot 96 Drive), Radio (Spice FM 'Adults in the Room'), TV (Mental Health interview), TikTok (@eddykimani), YouTube (Engage Kenya talk)
YouTube (DWJ Dialogues with Jagero), YouTube (Men's Mental Health Workshop), YouTube (TEDx-style talk), YouTube (Toxic Masculinity short-doc)

Instagram (Documentary launch caption), Podcast (CTA101 with Richard Njau), YouTube (A Girl Dad on a Mission, Ep. 1), YouTube (A Girl Dad on a Mission, Ep. 2), YouTube (A Girl Dad on a Mission, Finale)

snippets.context: [Eddy describes his rock-bottom moment after a failed county-government job, business losses, separation from wife, and being auctioned/evicted]
snippets.text: With no proper income coming through, I was kicked out of my rented house twice and got auctioned once. My friend Alan put me up the two times, and I had to do something unthinkable. At 39 years of age, I went back home, knocked on the door, and I told my mom, 'I failed.' It was extremely painful. On the surface, I kept a straight face, but deep down, I was burning. And several times, I wanted to let go.

snippets.context: [Eddy explains how taking the Nakuru county communications job led to long-distance marriage and financial strain]
snippets.text: Now, I had to supplement my salary, which had dipped after taking the county job. And like many other men would have done, I ventured into business. I ended up committing my full salary to service this loans, but the hole got even deeper. Everything I had planned to do at that time collapsed face-flat. So one thing led to another, I betrayed my marriage. I separated from my wife due to infidelity. Missed my children, and this killed me every single day and night. And I'm still trying to make up for that.

snippets.context: [Eddy's closing message to men in the Engage Kenya audience]
snippets.text: Because at the end of it all, when all is said and done, we must recognize that being perfect is a fairy tale. Failure, scarcity, and shortcomings are all part of being a man, a father, a husband. Openness, honesty, communication are key to your relationship with your partners, but sometimes, it can be difficult.

snippets.context: [Eddy continues his closing — frames self-pity as the central enemy for struggling men]
snippets.text: After all is said and done, self-pity is our number one enemy because it attracts to you helpless self-indulgence. And if that happens, focus on other things. Beware of excessive self-absorption and self-pity that robs us men the exercise of love, care, and many other things.

snippets.context: [Final closing message — Eddy directly tells men to show up for their children regardless of internal struggle]
snippets.text: After all is said and done, get close to your children. You don't have to feel strong and courageous to do this. Children need their fathers regardless what dad is going through inside. One of the gifts I'd like to give to my children is to demonstrate to them that you can recover from failure and keep moving.

snippets.context: [Opening of his second Engage Kenya talk — Eddy challenges the idea that men must commit to one fixed identity]
snippets.text: What if the biggest lie you've ever known is this: that you're only allowed to be one thing in this life? That if life gives you a bad hand, you just sit down and you accept it. That if you lose your title, your shine, your people, you stay there, quiet and finished. I believed that for a while, and if you met me back then, you would've believed it too. Here's what life taught me to understand. I was never built for just one version of myself.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes the phone call that crushed him after a TV screen test in 2017]
snippets.text: A few days later, the feedback came. It was a phone call and the person on the other end said, 'Eddy, you're too old to be back on TV.' Now, that cracked something inside me. It felt like somebody just punched me right in the gut. A gut that was already bleeding internally, bleeding from disappointment, rejection, brokenness. The punch basically added acid to injury. And for a while, I believed it. I believed that maybe Eddy was finished. Maybe this was it.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes how learning to make wire bonsai trees during his Mombasa rebuild became his therapy]
snippets.text: The trees never looked good like the pictures in my head, but they slowly became therapy for me. They did not care about my lost titles or my empty pockets. They did not judge me. They just stood there, stubborn, imperfect, just like me. Working that wire forced me to let go pride and ego. I had to trade applause for silence, for studio lights for dusty hands. I had to let go of the polished version of Eddy that I used to be. But in that shift, I found stillness, focus, and the quiet pride of creating something with my own hands.

snippets.context: [Eddy's closing rallying point in the Mombasa rebuild story]
snippets.text: So here's my truth: stay down if you have to, but do not unpack. When your life drops you, lie there if you have to, but when you stand up, stand up different, stand up weird.

snippets.context: [Eddy's closing call to men about how to show up for each other]
snippets.text: Being limitless is not just about ourselves, by the way. It's also how we show up for each other. Sometimes it's not about the big gestures, you know? But the small things: answering a call, giving a clear update, keeping your word. If somebody reaches out, pick up that call. If you're busy, let them know you'll call back and follow through. You might be their last bit of hope that day. Often they don't need solutions, just somebody who will listen without judgment.

snippets.context: [Eddy reflects on his own conduct during the period of marriage breakdown]
snippets.text: I had done them either out of ignorance um of course ego was part of it. Again making wrong decisions was like any other man would do you make wrong decisions from time to time in life. But I didn't have the tools to to deal with all that. I did not even know there were tools to deal with that to mend that. So I just wallowed in my space that I got into.

snippets.context: [Eddy explains why he didn't communicate with his wife during the dark period — his explicit framing of 'because I'm the man']
snippets.text: It got into a point where we we didn't talk as frequent as we were supposed to. Remember we have two sons so for obvious reasons we are supposed to be constantly talking. And a lot of that was to do with my ego and also thinking as much as I've got this problem uh I'll deal with it by myself. Little did I know I'm just digging my hole deeper and deeper deeper and deeper out of ignorance out of thinking I'm doing the right thing. And why am I thinking I'm doing the right thing? Because I'm the man. That was my thinking at the time. There's nothing wrong with with you believing you're doing the right thing in life but sometimes I think when it's not well informed when it's not well directed you'll you are bound to really have some toxic masculinity.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes how he ended up in Diani after his Nakuru contract ended and his marriage had collapsed]
snippets.text: But deep down what I was doing I was running away from my problems. I was running away from my responsibilities. Responsibility to myself as Eddie Kimani and responsibility of trying to mend things back at home. So I ended up in Ukunda. And it it is a time I wouldn't wish it for anybody and if anybody is going through it right now there's always a way a way of dealing with it. Now through that cycle I ended up now gigs ran out I was not doing anything.

snippets.context: [Eddy reveals — for the first time on this show — that he had decided to take his own life the night before meeting Peter at the bakery in Diani]
snippets.text: I call it my Damascus moment cause a night or two before that I I had concluded that I'll take away I'll take my own life. And I think what saved me was I I think I blacked out or something like that. Cause I woke up in the morning like okay I need to do something about this. I was so convinced I've had enough. I was so convinced I've reached the end. I was so convinced I don't need to go through this anymore. But remember all this time I'm just talking about me me me me. I'm forgetting there's this other side that I need also to deal with.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes the four-hour conversation with Peter — a stranger who became his lifeline]
snippets.text: I ended up sitting there talking to him for almost 3 hours. Talking about him he listened he didn't judge me. He didn't point anything at me he did not say Eddie you should have done this. The way we would normally do. Like why why did you do that. So it became a situation whereby I didn't know I was some form of therapy that I was going through. Cause what I needed at the time was just to open up without judgment and not to be criticized.

snippets.context: [Eddy makes one of his sharpest critiques of how Kenyan masculinity restricts when men are 'allowed' to feel]
snippets.text: I'd want to see a man cry if he really feels like crying. Unfortunately we live in a world where men show emotions in two scenarios. You could tell me if there's another one. I've only seen men crying at a funeral and during a sporting event.

snippets.context: [Eddy frames male self-care as central to mental health]
snippets.text: To be taken care of by just being self compassionate about about yourself. We need also to to to worry about ourselves. We need to take care of ourselves our well-being self care is very important.

snippets.context: [Eddy explains why men's heavy drinking and infidelity should be understood as depression coping mechanisms, not character flaws]
snippets.text: When you're in this space when you're depressed and and everything has just collapsed around you you look for coping mechanisms. And these coping mechanisms can be anything. It's not only alcohol. Coping mechanisms can go to the extreme. They are not doing it deliberately they are thinking they are just dealing they are just satisfying a need. But what they are fighting are some different type of elements that for them that seems like it's the only way to calm things down.

snippets.context: [Eddy critiques how Kenyans only associate mental health with the Mathare psychiatric institution]
snippets.text: When you talk about mental health you immediately think ah mathare. Mathare. And which is a very important institution in this country right now. You have a dark description on it. We don't have anything aspirational to talk. About taking care of your mental health. The way you walk into a gym and you see a poster of a built guy with a six pack you tell yourself I want to look like that. Why can I not see a photo of for example Eliud Kipchoge somewhere and say he's an aspiration to my the kind of mental health or mental well-being that I want in my life.

snippets.context: [Eddy explains why he is using barbershops as the centerpiece for his men's mental health initiative]
snippets.text: We want to collaborate with barbershops and barbers because this is one of the few if not the only spaces that we have as men where our guards are completely down. We have very authentic conversations. We talk about sports and we get very emotional. But why not see how we can weave around this space and let one of the conversations be about our own mental health, well-being? Because it's the only place where I can comfortably sit with a man with a blade on top of my head and feel not threatened. I trust my baba.

snippets.context: [Eddy explains the additional pressure of going through depression while being publicly known]
snippets.text: Sometimes when you're in the public domain you're treated more harshly because the spotlight is on you. So it becomes even harder to deal with some of these very glaring faux pas about normal things that happens to everybody everyday life. And people connect with you at that level. You are this guy.

snippets.context: [Eddy advises public figures going through hard times — humility is key]
snippets.text: Humbleness is very important because what happens that when you get elevated and you're given this sense of power and cause it's almost as having control of of something over you of over who you are and commanding attention here and there and that can get to your mind. It got into my mind. So you also have to learn how to be you know humility is very important especially in this day and age cause once you're knocked off we have you have more to deal with now in terms of how you've been exposed.

snippets.context: [Eddy answers Eric's question about how men should support friends going through crisis]
snippets.text: It could start by having interest about your friends. And not interest about where are we going to have our next beer not having interest of where where are we going to watch the next Formula 1 race. But it could go deep down to the point where you need to you you and there are ways of doing it. We also need to be able to reciprocate to it cause I might be a very very good friend and I'm concerned about you but you do not want to open up. And there's no way I'll be your friend and you're going through something and I if I'm honest with myself as your friend not want to help you.

snippets.context: [Eddy explains why he chose to share his story publicly at Engage rather than the safer New York-musical story]
snippets.text: It would be very selfish of you to be given such a platform and not share your story. It might impact one or two people, it might change their lives, it might make them see also me as a mirror of what they're going through in their lives. So I said okay, fine, let me twist this and just share my story. It was a painful pinch of salt to swallow because I had to make a decision to undress myself in the public.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes what stood out from his Engage talk]
snippets.text: Admitting that I failed. Kuaccept kwamba nilimake mistakes mob. Kuaccept and revealing that I failed my marriage. Admitting that I made wrong decisions about business. Admitting that I failed personally as a man, as a father. And I think also being an actor helped because you end up wearing masks, which is something that a lot of men are grappling with. The society is harsh on men. It's very, very harsh on men right now.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes how he reconciled with his eldest son after the separation]
snippets.text: For my children, whom I'd also failed for quite a while, the young kids, the firstborn is just turning, turned 15 this year. The first thing I did as part of reconciliation with him also was to learn to love him again, to learn who he was. And sitting him down and letting him see that Engage video. We sat down together and watched it. I just called him aside and told him, I have something I need to show you. And we sat. He watched and I apologized. The only thing that I really believe works when it comes to reconciliation is being honest with yourself and honest with the person you're trying to reconcile with.

snippets.context: [Eddy directly names the Swahili phrases used to enforce male emotional suppression]
snippets.text: Once you learn and embrace that especially for men, being weak is normal. It's good to be weak. We were raised in a society that tells us... be strong. Yes, man, brace yourself and toughen up. Men are not supposed to talk because even that idea of toughening up is repeated in different ways. You'll find situations where you want to share something with your fellow brother but you can't because you think they will... men don't do that. But that's what society has taught us. Men don't cry. Men don't show emotions. Men don't fail!

snippets.context: [Eddy directly addresses the gender-relations dimension of his message — refusing to scapegoat women]
snippets.text: It's hard for me to say this, but men should understand that women are not to blame because they are rising up. They are rising up. But men should not feel that they are being weak because women are rising up.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes the wave of women asking him to help their depressed husbands — and his caution]
snippets.text: I'm getting also feedback from ladies asking me to help their husbands because they feel the husband does not want to be helped. Now that one you have to be careful because again I can't start engaging somebody who may not want or is not ready to be helped. He may ask, why are you talking to my wife, what business do you have with her?

snippets.context: [Eddy lists the things that helped him beyond the Engage talk]
snippets.text: I realized forgiveness is one key thing. I had to forgive myself. I had to ask for forgiveness from my wife, from my family. I had to of course approach God in a different way because we cannot talk about straightening up your life without involving God. And that really has helped me a lot being closer to Him. And also what has helped me a lot is understanding that it's my kids who need me more than the other way around.

snippets.context: [Eddy frames his recovery as a gift to his sons specifically]
snippets.text: One of the best gifts and I keep on saying this is to make my kids realize that when you fail, it's possible to stand up again and move. It's a gift I'd like to give them every day because they are growing, they're boys. But I'd like also for them to see that it's possible to be an upright man, to be a man who's respected in the society and just really embody what a real man is all about. I have that responsibility now as a father and I've dedicated my life now to do that.

snippets.context: [Eddy warns of what happens to men who continue suppressing emotions]
snippets.text: We will continue having broken men. We'll continue having broken men, men who are not ready to because I also believe that men will become so numbed in struggling to keep that status that you're a man, you don't need to cry. So we've gone numb that we are not able to show feelings because we've been told by the society that you as a man is not supposed to cry. A man is not supposed to say when he's down. A man is not supposed to reach out and look for help.

snippets.context: [Eddy's closing words on the show]
snippets.text: Men, it's good to be weak, it's good to fail, because you can still stand up and try again. And it's okay not to be okay.

snippets.context: [Eddy responds to data showing 147 of 177 reported Kenyan suicides in 2018 were men]
snippets.text: I'm a strong believer of the fact that women have very strong coping systems. Unfortunately, men, even amongst ourselves, it's a challenge even coming out and saying, I need help, or reaching out. Because as he said, we are not supposed to show our weaknesses. Even some reports indicate that there are men who would be happier going for a prostate cancer test than going to a psychiatrist.

snippets.context: [Eddy explains why men cannot share their emotional reality with each other]
snippets.text: We are wired, of course by the society, how we've been brought up, not to show these emotions. Unfortunately, we do not know how to be vulnerable. A big general, and a very sizable number of us men do not understand how to embrace, and that's why you get the case of toxic masculinity coming into place. And you will be suppressing all these emotions. And these emotions should actually be working for us. Our vulnerability should is supposed to work for us, not against us.

snippets.context: [Eddy reflects on how his media background made his depression worse]
snippets.text: I was coming back from a background where I was already in the public eye. So I've got this pressure to stay and look in a particular way. And as a man who's been exposed to that space, you will do everything and anything to always look normal. No matter how much you're going through in life. So it was a situation where I thought I'm dealing with it and this is normal, this is how it's supposed to be. And the way I'm dealing with it is the best way I can. So there was a lot of course selfishness, the ego had grown the size of an elephant because you think you cannot tell me how to deal with my problems.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes his depression and the moment a 'flicker' of sense came through]
snippets.text: Depression is, which is what I went through, is like a prison with no key, and the thoughts are the lock. So you keep on fighting with these thoughts constantly, in your space, that prison. I wish I knew this then. That there is a way I can do this and that is by asking for help. Saying, and raising my hand and saying, you know what? I don't think I'm okay.

snippets.context: [Eddy connects male suicide rates to economic pressures and the provider-role expectation]
snippets.text: Men trying to deliver their bit as men. It's not necessarily that I love gambling. It's not necessarily that I love betting. But you find we, men at that stage now, jobs are not available. There is an avenue here which I feel fine, it's a quick, easy, according to you as a man, to make money. Because now it looks like an easy way out, but now unfortunately, some of us men are not willing to go out there and do, and toil for actual, you know, just do some honest work.

snippets.context: [Eddy responds to a young TikToker (Sparki) who claimed any man over 35 should own a car. Eddy addresses him directly.]
snippets.text: Having a car does not mean you've made it in life, and not having one does not mean somebody is struggling or lazy. Do you know that life is not a straight line, my friend? And people at different stages of life. The same way you are proud of your win today. Somebody else is working tirelessly there tomorrow. Young man, ego is loud. Growth is quiet. Please do not let your achievements turn into a weapon to shame others. A car is metal. It moves, and that's it. It does not define who you are or how successful you are.

snippets.context: [Eddy responds to an online discussion about men disappearing from clubs, festivals, and church events]
snippets.text: Men are not missing. Men are evolving. Men are quietly walking away from the performance culture, from chaos, from spaces that demand that they must prove their self, that they must prove their fun, that they must prove their faith or their worth. They're not hiding, they're healing. They're learning that peace is louder than music, that purpose-led life feels better than applause. To the women who may feel offended please don't be. We don't hate women. We are just learning as men to love ourselves properly so that we can love you better. We are not ghosting society. We are grounding ourselves.

snippets.context: [Eddy opens his Engage talk by recounting the morning his face went numb during a meeting]
snippets.text: It was about 8:30 a.m. thereabouts. I got a weird taste in my mouth. It was metallic. Shortly after investigating with my tongue, I realized my the left side of my face was getting numb. Before I could find out what was going on, my left eye couldn't blink normally. I was checked. My blood pressure was through the roof. The neurologist did a few tests, and he told me what I was suffering from: Bell's palsy. I was shell-shocked. Many thoughts ran through my mind. Is this the moment that I would implode from within? Is this what my life had become?

snippets.context: [Eddy describes how his friend network sorted itself during his breakdown]
snippets.text: My mom was my beacon of light through this rough waters that I had thrown myself into. She was there for me. Some of my friends walked away into the horizon, they went. I came to terms with that. But I need to appreciate and acknowledge friends and strangers who stood by me, held my hand in this dark moments.

snippets.context: [Eddy recounts walking into the Diani bakery and meeting Peter — the foundational story he tells across multiple platforms]
snippets.text: So one morning, I walked into a bakery and I sat down, and this guy behind me greeted me, and he told me his name was Peter and he introduced himself as the owner of the bakery. He recognized me as that guy of TV. 'Ah, you are that guy from TV, eh?' I wanted the earth to open, I fall in. This was my Damascus moment. But that time I had spent almost four hours conversing with Peter, and he went on his knees and prayed for me. Something that shocked me. He told me I needed to go back home. I needed to go back to my family. He pushed me every day as we became friends. But unfortunately, the demons kept on winning this battle all through.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes the trip back to Nairobi after his wife asked him to attend their son's school meeting]
snippets.text: When I go to Nairobi, this was the tail end of a vicious cycle that I had gone through. The pain, the heart that I had caused, nothing mattered anymore but my children, my wife, my family, and putting my life back in order again. It was not easy. Reconciliation between me and my wife was work in progress. My children had... I had to learn how to love them again, be curious about them, and understand that it's me they need more and not the other way around.

snippets.context: [Eddy's closing line at Engage]
snippets.text: Be grateful that you don't look like the problems and the challenges that we go through. I'm not proud of what I have done, but I'm proud of what I've become and I can be through the lessons that I have learned. There's more to you than your temporary setbacks. You've got what it takes.

snippets.context: [After being told 'you're too old' for TV, Eddy reframes the rejection]
snippets.text: Somewhere in that bruise, a new thought took root. When somebody tells you you're too old, you're too late, you're too much, what they really mean is this: you have outgrown their imagination, not yours.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes the moment of crashing in a friend's empty servants' quarter after losing his marketing job and marriage cracking again in 2020]
snippets.text: I crashed in a friend's empty servants' quarter. So one mattress, four walls, and one voice in my head asking myself a question: 'Eddy, is this how the story ends?' Nobody was coming. The old Eddy was not coming to rescue me. He had served his time.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes the unexpected phone call from a man's WhatsApp group friend who had just emptied his Mombasa house]
snippets.text: Then out of the blue, a friend from a men's WhatsApp group called me. He also lived in Mombasa and he was just checking on me. I told him my plan, we both laughed. And then he told me, 'Eddy, I just moved out of my house, and it's empty.' You know, sometimes life conspires for you in ways that you cannot even understand. He took me to the house, I met the caretaker and we hit it off. He booked it for me. No deposit, no tricks, just trust.

snippets.context: [Eddy on what Mombasa actually was for him]
snippets.text: Now Mombasa was not a beach holiday for me. Let me kill that fantasy right now. Mombasa humbled me. I had to change my perspective from Mombasa being this holiday destination and remember that this will now be my home where I will live and find a way to survive and live in.

snippets.context: [Eddy warns against romanticizing the coast move]
snippets.text: But don't get me wrong, Mombasa has sweet distractions, cheap escapes, nice smelling traps that will drain you dry. Some days they nearly won, but most days I kept on choosing my new path.

snippets.context: [Eddy explains his shift into mental health advocacy work]
snippets.text: I took my scars and turned them outwards. I trained as a mental health advocate and a trainer because it was selfish to keep holding all these hard-learned lessons. It was selfish of me through the lived experience I had.

snippets.context: [Eddy parallels physical and mental health practices in childhood]
snippets.text: We went to the gym we go to the gym and take care of our physical health. And fortunately we grew up in an environment where we are taught wash your hands before you eat. Wash your hands when you leave the toilet. But we are not taught how to take care of our mental fitness our mental hygiene. So we are now in a space where we are trying to embrace and understand mental health.

snippets.context: [Eddy explains why men kept reaching out after his Engage talk]
snippets.text: By the way my story is not unique. It is not unique in any particular way. Is just that you were given the... I was given a people do it's just that I was given a higher platform to share my life. So what happened is that the feedback I got got me into this space now where people are reaching out for help. Eddie I'm going through this I'm going through the same situation you're going through and I need help.

snippets.context: [Eddy explains how taking the Nakuru job meant losing his media identity and forced him into business on the side]
snippets.text: It meant a lot of adjusting. You know you're no longer on people's lips you're no longer in front of people in their TV screens you're no longer on people's minds. So a lot of things started falling off. My pay was not the same as I I was getting at at the station I was at at the time. And then here I am with a new challenge in front of me. I made decisions which did not go the right way with me. I I failed my marriage being away from my family. I I got into debt because I was trying to supplement.

snippets.context: [Eddy on how he made his series of bad decisions during the dark season]
snippets.text: I made wrong decisions. Wrong business decisions that's why I got into debt. I got distracted that's why my marriage collapsed. I let down my fatherhood responsibilities being away. So it was really a collapse of everything and then at the middle of it I got something called Bell's palsy. So all that all that put together everything just came tumbling down. I just I don't know how I got through it.

snippets.context: [A caller named Mathew describes saving a friend who had been planning to jump from Tower One — Eddy responds, validating the listening intervention]
snippets.text: Very very. Thank you Matthew. Fantastic Matthew asante. [In response to Mathew's story of saving a friend by listening: it's good to hear that actually — you did something very important which is to listen to him.]

snippets.context: [After describing the barbershop project, Eddy expands the model to include bartenders]
snippets.text: Another guy I always come across my mind is that guy at the bar. You know, the guy at the counter. He really listens to many stories from many many people. So imagine if you mold that. That's also another way, another avenue. Because men, we will never be vulnerable when we are outside our comfort. And when you are in that baba's seat, you sometimes even blackout.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes his consulting work with companies on mental health]
snippets.text: I'm also focused on talking to employers through their companies by providing lived experience mental health training where I collaborate with consulting psychologists. I collaborate with psychiatrists where we come and do talks to your staff cause the workplace is another space that we need to start having employers understanding you need to invest more in mental health and well-being in that office. Companies are spending so much money on safety and security which is good, it's fantastic. But health is also a good investment.

snippets.context: [Eddy's prescription for cultural change at the highest levels]
snippets.text: Another solution or a direction I think that would help this country and the world. If we have our leaders talking about their mental health. It would go a long way. A CEO waking up in the morning and saying I went through these tribulations. I am in touch with the problems I am and I understand what you're going through. Leaders need to start talking. About mental health.

snippets.context: [Eddy describes the experience of pouring out his story on the Engage stage]
snippets.text: I went onto that stage and poured my soul out. It was therapeutic. It was a load off my shoulder talking about myself and my story and what I've been through. And for me, it was a revelation because as men, we rarely talk, we rarely open up. So it was also an opportunity for me to share with like-minded men who also attend Engage and see if I could have an impact in also telling a story by hearing a story from a man and be honest and be brutal, brutal with myself.

snippets.context: [Eddy reframes failure for men]
snippets.text: People should embrace that aspect of being vulnerable; I am known that way, and failure is part of the process. It's part of the process, it's actually one of the key ingredients for success. You just need to make failure your ally. You just need to make failure, don't hate it. Stand up, dust yourself and move.

snippets.context: [Eddy responds to whether being a man is harder for the current generation]
snippets.text: Iko challenged. The dynamics I mean by we are now bombarded by this whole internet and digital. Haikuwapo time yetu. Right now there's so much pressure in terms of where they're getting information from that it might not be just one channel to pick up information. The other aspect about the girl child being too empowered. And I don't think there's anything like too empowered. Yes, they're being empowered, but I think also the boy child also has been forgotten a little bit. So there's that aspect also that is making it a challenge for boys to graduate into manhood.

snippets.context: [Eddy on whether theater can play a role in male mental health conversations]
snippets.text: It's one of the best even back in the days in the back I'm talking about years back. The theater was used to communicate stories about the societies. It was not just used for entertainment. It was used to be the mirror of the society. There is a lot of potential. It's just a matter of getting together and realizing yes, this is what we need to put together as a story, as a conversation, and put it in a package and put it on TV, put it on theater, put it on YouTube.

snippets.context: [Eddy critiques how media narratives blame all men for the actions of a few]
snippets.text: Most of the bad things happen and are pinned on men. Unfortunately. So also the messaging out there about men. And I know there are men out there who are doing really great things and if they just covered and the narrative is also channeled towards showing what good men are doing out there because it becomes a situation now the society gets you know with the point of view that men are really, really bad. Unfortunately, a few rotten potatoes... apples. Potatoes. Is it potatoes, apples, potatoes, even samaki waoze wote.

snippets.context: [Eddy and co-guest Kiarii discuss how men are their own worst critics — Eddy validates and extends this]
snippets.text: Remember, we also now have our own self-expectations. So you're adding that expectation, Eddie you should have been here, Eddie you should have used that degree to achieve this. Look at them now, they are laughing at you. So you also are dealing with both sides. So you're dealing with your own self-expectations and trying to make these other people happy. And you are constantly, as a man, being badgered and you're also badgering yourself to reach these expectations or even just to pass, to supersede them. So it's both ways. In and out.

snippets.context: [Onyango facilitating a men's mental health workshop. He is debunking the assumption that African men's emotional suppression is a uniquely African problem, sharing what he learned visiting Canadian mental health clinics.]
snippets.text: Our assumption often is to a black man is new it's new to us an African man is new but you will be very surprised to know it's actually men on a global fear yes it's not just as it's men on a global sphere we fear talking we don't know how to talk it's not just us. I got a mental health Fellowship to go to Canada a while back and I was visiting the mental health clinics and I'm telling you bro men are not there yet they are not there. Just because they they are white men. So if for and even the idea about being in those kind of spaces made me realize when we're in Africa here we think we are the worst we think we are going through the most we think we don't know it's not true it really isn't true. The world out there people are struggling the same way we are struggling.

snippets.context: [Onyango is explaining how prolonged trauma affects the brain — particularly for men exposed to constant danger or stress]
snippets.text: You can't process emotions properly and you can't think rational all right because your body is really just trying to keep you alive because you are sensing danger too often sensing danger too often get what I mean. All right this is the epitome of mental health all right because your daily experiences are connected so your body moves according to how your environment is. If you feel safe your nerves are relaxed, right; if you feel in danger your nerves are tight.

snippets.context: [Continuation — Onyango explaining how chronic male trauma manifests as violence and irrational behavior]
snippets.text: When you are in traumatic situations or traumatic situations for a long time it becomes chronic it means this part of your brain is overworking the part of your brain that just wants to keep you alive so it means you don't often think well or think rationally you can do things or say things that you regret or you want to be violent and stuff like that.

snippets.context: [Onyango reflects on facilitating the workshop and what it means for Kenyan men]
snippets.text: I was the facilitator of the relate men's mental health Workshop it was one of the best days of my life why because I love talking to men I love seeing and hearing men talk to each other and today again we just witnessed how important how valuable how significant it is when men come together to talk to one another about each other. I am also quite passionate about continuing with these kind of workshops because it emulates or it models to men like me that it's actually possible to be vulnerable to one another specifically as men because it goes a long way to making us know that creating Healthy Communities with each other is one of the ways that we are going to continue feeling loved on this Earth that most days we feel very alone.

snippets.context: [Onyango explains his sex-stories podcast 'Nothing Underneath' — describing the kinds of stories people share, including a queer Kenyan man's account of secretly experimenting in school]
snippets.text: For me that documentation is important because as a society we have very nuanced about sex we pretend so much that this is not something we are ready to talk about or to do but we have a very scandalous relationship with the thing.

snippets.context: [Onyango contrasts his early childhood with Jagero's — describing the years when his father was emotionally present]
snippets.text: I remember the first seven years of my life were very good very very good. My dad we were really good friends he used to have this thing where he'd put me on the living room table and put on his Sanyo radio for those who are old enough who can remember Sanyo and put on lingala music or sukus and he tell me to dance for him. And so dancing became sort of like a love language in in my home whenever I saw my father I knew it's time to dance you know and we listen to a lot of music at home he'd buy me Taifa Leo and put me on his lap and tell me and show me how to read.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes the pivotal moment when his family moved and his father took a private-school job — the entry point of money, women and violence]
snippets.text: Then we moved from kangela and that's when the hell began because he was teaching in a government school and then we went for the first time he got work in a private school and uh you see that came with money and then it also came with women and then now the violence began.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes the conditions of his Huruma home and how parents fighting in tight spaces affects children]
snippets.text: We lived in very dingy homes man you know the whole day because the window there's another plot on the other side you know there was no space at all PL those men used to fight man I don't know and it is in the same place that you boys are there with your friends playing Bano playing football there is sewage going there was chaos everywhere and at the same time my parents would fight a lot.

snippets.context: [Onyango recounts the central trauma of his life — the night his father accused him of stealing a Bill Cosby novel and burned his hand with kerosene]
snippets.text: He's looking for this book and he seems not to be finding it if something is not in the four corners of a single room surely it's not there. He starts beating me up but then this night was different. He gets out he comes back to the house he comes back with the wire like a meter of that telephone copper wire and he strips me of my clothes. So he is whipping my back and he's telling me to produce the book. My father used to beat you with his soul my friend all the anger that he carried in his body comes to this moment.

snippets.context: [Continuation of the kerosene incident — Onyango's father returns with rope, kerosene jerrycan, and matches]
snippets.text: I lie at other times but today I'm lying about something that I actually didn't do so that I can be left alone and I say at some point I say okay I took it. He looks at me with his bloodshot eyes and he says I'll do something to you that you'll never forget. He walks out he comes back with a size rope as if he'd planned all this and he goes into the kitchen he comes back with the Jeran paraffin Jeran maut those days and he comes with a matchbox. And he ties my hands ties my hands he opens the paraffin Jeran pours some of the paraffin on the lid smears the paraffin on my hand lights up the match stick and puts the flame on my hand.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes the immediate aftermath of the burning — and the lasting psychological wound]
snippets.text: I have a scar if you've been seeing it so that's the scar this is the scar and I think my brain blocked the pain of that specific moment because I could I cannot remember it what I know is I felt like something had been broken inside me that I didn't have a name for. I was never the same I could not walk for two weeks. I became suicidal.

snippets.context: [After the burning, Onyango describes how he changed personality completely]
snippets.text: I began fearing the world because I imagined if these people at home can do this to me what about strangers man that's a 10-year-old brain asking what about strangers you see so I withdrew completely became very quiet RX who was very outgoing and you know playmaking and all that I just became quiet because now it's about protecting myself now you don't know what's the next thing that will happen.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes trying to physically intervene in his parents' fights]
snippets.text: And in a single room when people are fighting Jagero it's crazy because these people are bundling each other corner to corner to corner it's like wrestling you're just watching WWE there you know and sometimes you become collateral because sometimes I would try to get in between the fights to get my dad away from my mom and he'd just throw me out like like some fly you know.

snippets.context: [Onyango on how the household carried on as if nothing had happened the next day]
snippets.text: Life went back to normal like the previous night like nothing had happened and that's how things used to be in the home they fight very badly tonight nothing homework nothing had happened. Isn't that a horrible way of living?

snippets.context: [Onyango introduces the clinical concept of complex post-traumatic stress to explain his childhood]
snippets.text: I went through something in Psychology is called complex post-traumatic stress which is experiencing stress on different layers. So on top of the witnessing violence between my parents there was violence that was also being meted upon me there was violence I was seeing in my neighborhood because especially in huruma I witnessed so many young people being gunned down by police point blank in the evenings so you'd hear a gunshot at 6:00 p.m. man you know and some dude has just gone like that and his meat is just all over the place. The trauma of poverty all that chaos growing up in.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes how Nairobi itself triggers him decades later]
snippets.text: I still have very active poverty trauma. Every time I look at this city I just see problems. They are parts of this city that when I go to my blood just go cold. I remember them very very clearly like Mudura I don't like going there Mudura just reminds me of utter powerlessness and poverty. There is a bridge that's going over I don't like going to that bridge. Yes that bridge reminds me of very dark dark times. There are the times of no food there the times of no school fees no pocket money despair apathy despair sickness it's just bad bad bad.

snippets.context: [Onyango explains why poverty-trauma stays with men into adulthood]
snippets.text: Mick was talking about reparenting myself and I think I need to reparent myself again about poverty because if you grow up without anything. If you are if you a friend of the bottle then whatever you do in life whether you are whatever maybe if you're a billionaire or a millionaire but yes you just remain as a family no nothing so you just grow up in lack everyday lack everyday lack everyday lack you come to the city. And I think if you get into your adulthood without luck it destroys you because you have embodied it you know it's in your body now you actually believe this is your identity. Sometimes even you can get a lot of money and it's sits in the bank and you're afraid of using it. Or you squander it because you don't believe you you you're supposed to have money.

snippets.context: [Onyango recounts the 2015 confrontation he organized with his parents — and the pivotal statement his father made]
snippets.text: When it was my turn to talk to dad he told me things that I I think also even retraumatized me. But then as we were talking this man asked me a question that also changed my life he said I don't come to you with my problems because you think I'm weak you know and that's a very powerful statement to come from any man and that's that line sank into me like wine my friend. This man who I've lived with 25 years first of all what makes him think that I don't already know his weaknesses you see. What is this idea of him showing up as a perfect parent and he's already making the mistakes and we are suffering from them is this denial or what's going on around here?

snippets.context: [Continuation — Onyango describes the cognitive shift after his father's confession]
snippets.text: And it's the first time that I recognize this man is also not okay there's something else going on and I stop looking at him barely as my dad and start seeing him as another wounded child just as I was wounded you know. And my quest changes because now I stop centering him in my life because always it was about oh dad we need you to change we need you to do that blah blah blah. And I realized it's not going to happen any soon I need to find other ways to understand what this life is all about.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes his first depression at 16 and how nobody recognized the symptoms]
snippets.text: What happened at when I was 10 caught up with me by the time I was 15 I started experiencing depression in high school school and uh eventually I was expelled from school had all these withdrawal symptoms I don't want to read I I didn't wash my clothes I'd steal people's clothes steal people's monies my life was just in despair at 16 years old. Now I was running away from home I became a street boy at some point I was hanging out on Koinange streets here with sex workers I was shoplifting from supermarkets. But all these were symptoms of what's going on at home and nobody was picking this up. So you're being seen as a problematic adolescent but really these are symptoms of trauma that you you are exhibiting in your behavior and your brain is actually suffering but nobody seems to understand the language of this thing.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes coming out publicly about his depression in 2017]
snippets.text: This the second time I fell into depression and I was also suicidal I was very public about it 2017 early 2017 and that also really changed my life because being a Storyteller being an artist you've known me all these years now I started ping uh putting things together and speaking up about it. There are so many people who reached out to me saying they were going through the same things they feeling depressive they were feeling social and I was like how come I don't hear about this thing in everyday life nobody's talking about it.

snippets.context: [Onyango explains the technical definition of trauma]
snippets.text: Essentially trauma is actually very wide but at the very basic level it is anything that happens to your body or your mind that destabilizes your normal body functioning. Trauma comes from the Greek word for wound. So that's where medicine got the word Trauma from it means wound. So a wound definitely means there has been a destabilization of normal function because something has been opened up some wires have been cut or there's overexposure to danger.

snippets.context: [Onyango lists the different forms of trauma]
snippets.text: On very different levels there is either physical trauma there is either religious trauma or emotional trauma Financial trauma um sexual abuse trauma um child neglect all those things are forms of trauma.

snippets.context: [Onyango explains what PTSD specifically means]
snippets.text: With post-traumatic stress really it's the long-term effects the adverse effects of experiencing very difficult situations when you where you felt helpless and you were exposed to danger and shock but did not have the proper support. So even these nightmares that you talk about and dreams that and fear of going to certain places all those are forms of PTSD that never got the support to heal.

snippets.context: [Onyango walks through the three brain regions to explain how trauma changes male behavior]
snippets.text: The brain has many parts but for the purpose of the conversation I will speak about three major ones so you have something called the hindbrain the midbrain and the forebrain. The hindbrain is also sometimes called The Reptilian Brain it's the lowest part of the human brain. The mid brain is where we process our emotions and we store our memories. The for brain is also called the the thinking brain or the frontal lobe so the frontal lobe usually it takes the most energy in your brain and in that part of the brain you use it to to solve problems to create.

snippets.context: [Onyango explains how chronic stress in men leads to seemingly senseless violence — the bus-stop killings, the Manchester United fights]
snippets.text: When you are Overexposed to trauma it means you are constantly on survival mode trying to protect yourself. That means the part of your brain that is essential for your survival is overworking which is The Reptilian Brain. It is overworking that means the other parts of the brain's energy won't be going there for you to use. So they are affected that means you can't process emotions properly and you can't think rationally. You've heard stories of an Ana leuka or you hear stories on the news somebody killed the other because of some 20 Shilling debt or Manchester United Arsenal game. They can't think rationally they've been overexposed to to stress.

snippets.context: [Onyango explains why our culture misreads adolescent boys' behavior]
snippets.text: Children at that age when they are overly traumatized sometimes experience withdrawal symptoms or they get into drugs or they act out a lot at home whatever and it happens a lot during this adolescent age this is when some of these things start manifesting but our culture takes it as this is just adolescence. We often need to remember that when a child is developing especially when when they get to from 10 age of 10 to like around 16 17 those are very critical years the first 25 years of a human being's life are extremely essential because this is the time their brains are developing their core values are developing their personalities are developing.

snippets.context: [Onyango on what parents should do to recognize trauma in their children]
snippets.text: I think the first point is for the parents themselves to look at themselves first because I think many of the times we are living in a very complex country. This is a country that has had historical trauma that we don't talk about we have a lot of religious trauma that we don't talk about that we bring home to our kids we have a lot of poverty trauma right that we are every day running away from. We have a lot of gender trauma yeah uh who men are supposed to be women children all that stuff going on queer people. So in most cases parents are first the ones who are traumatized before they can start looking out for trauma in their children they need to first look at themselves and that's one of the tenets of conscious parenting.

snippets.context: [Onyango compares Kenyan parenting to colonial mindset]
snippets.text: Many times we look at children as subjects in this country the same way the colonial white people looked at Africans as subjects right so if you are looking at your child and you're thinking this is somebody who is subject to me and not a full human being then chances are higher that you're going to abuse this person and that's what happens a lot.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes the typical signs of trauma in children]
snippets.text: In children many times when you feel the space at home is contracted and you cannot speak you cannot express yourself what children do is is they shut down they withdraw they look for attention in other ways. So you might find a child doing something so silly and it acts the parents so much and what the parent again does is to beat but what the child is saying in other ways is I need you to see me. Children want to be seen. Sometimes children will get into Bad Company so that they can get that sense of belonging that is not there at home or in their neighborhood or in their culture.

snippets.context: [Onyango explains why there isn't one way to heal from trauma]
snippets.text: I am really not of the idea that there is one way of dealing with trauma because you know even here we can be in one house we can may have grown up in one house but the way I respond to trauma is different from way the way you will respond to trauma. We have very different responses to to what's going on in in the moment right that also means getting out of this thing in terms of healing also looks different from for different people. So there are people who might need medical therapy because of how sometimes damaged the brain is there are people who might need just to talk about it which is talk therapy.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes somatic and body-based therapies]
snippets.text: There is something else called somatic experiencing and this is what is mostly recommended for people who've gone through a lot of PTSD where your body is involved in the process of the healing so you take up exercises physical exercises you take up dancing you take up something you call body mapping you take up hiking you take up activities that engage your body.

snippets.context: [Onyango's central thesis on what works most]
snippets.text: But also I must emphasize this the most sustainable tool is community community is the most sustainable tool for trauma healing because outside therapy outside all these other interventions if you do not have community to share life with it becomes very difficult to heal in isolation. The reason they exist is so that you can connect back with your body and your nervous system so that you can be able to open up again and love and share and intimate with other people because our nervous system which is the parts that are also diely affected by trauma cannot connect when they are closed up.

snippets.context: [Onyango on how trauma manifests in male workplace behavior]
snippets.text: Take a very simple example in the workplace if your boss was somebody who had a difficult childhood in the sense that they were violated and they were not paid enough emotional attention they could be people who want to show how big they are. You ever met these people who always want to prove how big they are. Those guys those are that's a trauma response.

snippets.context: [Onyango lists adult masculine trauma symptoms]
snippets.text: Others are violent to their partners and their children. They're shouting all the time which is different from what you do. Others don't communicate they don't know how to communicate their feelings why because when they were younger it was not they were never given space to express themselves so they don't know how to express their needs they don't know how to express their emotions. Another way is self-sabotage that you don't believe you can be in the places that you're in. Some people don't know how to take care of themselves some people overshop some people overdrink some people over rely on drugs and substances to get by — anything that gives them dopamine.

snippets.context: [Onyango explains how he and Jagero playing FIFA together is itself a coping mechanism]
snippets.text: When we are meeting up for FIFA the fight is in the pitch you know I'm not coming to fight Jagero. I'm coming to release pressure to keep my head level. I am getting off some steam from my body and that is a very important component of of coping right because connection is the best way to cope because you go back to yourself you come back to an equilibrium.

snippets.context: [Onyango on creating anti-traumatic spaces in everyday relationships]
snippets.text: I often advocate for authenticity in individualism right where if I know I can be who I am and you let me be that we will have very limited space to traumatize each other. If all the time you I would shut you down when you're about to score a goal I tell you ah keep quiet don't talk don't shout then you learn that oh I cannot be myself around this person then you shut down. Now imagine that happening to a child what does that do they actually believe that's all their reach to life it's to keep quiet.

snippets.context: [Onyango critiques the abstract 'society' that men feel pressured to perform for]
snippets.text: When you go down to it Society is actually an illusion. It's an illusion because I I fear Society I want to impress Society who is society who is society. Is it your neighbor is it the manga? They don't know you so you danced again on on on. Society is an illusion.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes his father's contradictory life — drunk on Saturday, reading announcements at Catholic Mass on Sunday]
snippets.text: My dad was a very religious man in the Catholic church. So it was so interesting on Saturday nights, he'll be brought home by his friends because he couldn't walk on his own because he's drunk. And on Sunday afternoon, when we go to church together, he's reading announcements at the pulpit. That was very confusing for me.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes his father's violent rage — using the phrase that recurs across his content]
snippets.text: I was used to being beaten. My dad would beat you with his soul. Like all the problems he's going through in his life come to that one moment and he beats you up like it's it's do or die.

snippets.context: [Onyango on the immediate aftermath of the kerosene burning]
snippets.text: I changed completely. I started withdrawing because now home now stopped feeling like home. It even started feeling like school. It became a war zone. You know, I'm here with people I can't trust anymore. Mom couldn't protect me. Dad is being abusive. My uncle is just there. Everybody's watching me being abused and I can't tell anybody. So I just became quiet.

snippets.context: [Onyango's central diagnosis of why men in adult relationships expect emotional labor from women]
snippets.text: We rely on women on emotional labor because we were separated from our emotions from a young young age. It was beaten out of us. Don't cry. Be hard, meaning so you become an adult and all of a sudden you're in a relationship and this woman expects you to show your emotions. You don't know how to show your emotions. She don't know how to open up.

snippets.context: [Onyango articulates his self-rescue framework — what to do when childhood didn't give you what you deserved]
snippets.text: I deserved a healthy home. I deserved healthy parents. I deserved love. And since I couldn't get those things as a child, I had to learn how to give them to myself. It meant I had to stop my brain from simulating the violence I was taught, the violence I was conditioned to and create a new brain for myself.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes summoning his parents to a meeting at age 24-25 — calling it 'suicidal']
snippets.text: I took it upon myself to call my parents for a meeting, which is very suicidal. Here I was like, 'Hey, you people, me I'm tired. I'm tired of this skills. I'm tired of the fights. I just want a peaceful home. And I'm now an adult. I want a peaceful life.' Now, being a firstborn in the house, I think I was just forced to grow up so quick. Sometimes it felt like I was actually parenting my own parents.

snippets.context: [Onyango on his father's confession]
snippets.text: He said, 'I don't come to you with my problems because you think I'm weak.' Okay? So, you know, in my mind, I'm like, this dude actually thinks I view him as a perfect person just because he never comes to me with these problems. And on the other side, I'm thinking, how much pain must he have gone through or been going through for him to feel he can't express himself to me, his child, his firstborn child?

snippets.context: [Onyango describes his ongoing confusion about his father as a person]
snippets.text: I had been asking so many questions about dad because he seemed like a very strange person to me. You know, the violence we grew up in was intensely insane.

snippets.context: [Onyango on the moment his father pivoted from search to accusation]
snippets.text: It seemed he had misplaced this text and I'm like Dad no I've I've not seen that book. He asks you this question which if an African parent asks you you have nowhere else to go. You know can the house help steal a book? Your uncle can he steal a book? You're the only person going to school here. Then he's like produce that book and it was interesting how suspicion moved to accusation.

snippets.context: [Detailed reconstruction of the burning scene]
snippets.text: He stripped me off my clothes. And he would lash at my back, lash at my back, lash at my back with this copper. I was 10. Very tiny body. I was 10 years old. And in 15 minutes my body was frailing and frailing and frailing. Because I wasn't sure how much this man was willing to go. Now, this book he was looking for belongs to a student he used to tutor. So, of course, it was a very important book to him. And the fact that he couldn't find it definitely the stakes were high. And I was almost the easiest target for his accusation at that moment in time.

snippets.context: [Onyango on the moment he decided to falsely confess]
snippets.text: I remember my little child intelligence telling me, 'You know, we are not so sure about this guy tonight. So, just like this once, you know, you lie at the times for things you did, but today, just like you, you didn't do this thing, but you actually, you know, just say you did it.' So, I said I took the book.

snippets.context: [Onyango describing the eye contact during the burning]
snippets.text: He was, do you know these things like you watch them in movies like somebody burning you and they're looking at you? That's exactly what happened. I remember that. He was looking at me and he was he was putting the flame here on my hand because of a book that I supposedly stole.

snippets.context: [Onyango on how the 10-year-old wound came back at 16]
snippets.text: Now, this was me 10 years. So, six years later, now the traumas had started showing up on my body. I was in form two. My depression is starting to show up. None of us knew this was depression. What would happen is, you know, I was taken to form one in a school very far away from home. So, I lost my sense of belonging.

snippets.context: [Onyango details the form-two breakdown]
snippets.text: In that school, I stopped bathing. In fact, I remember I had this long streak of nine days without bathing. Just me going to class, going to the pitch, coming back, najipangusa. That was my life. And then I stopped going to class. Now, I was hanging out in the bathrooms by myself. And then I started stealing things because I wasn't doing my own laundry. I would steal my friends' clothes. And then I had steal money. And then, you know, I was busted and expelled.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes one of the worst fights he witnessed and intervened in]
snippets.text: The more money he started earning, the more violent he became at home. I remember even one night, you know, my mom forgot to do something for the village they have in Uganda. And my dad pounced on her back and started pulling her hair until this hairline started bleeding. You know, and that night I pounced on him trying to separate his grip from mom's hairline, because she was kneeling on the carpet because of something so small. And I felt like I wanted to kill him that night. It's just that I'm thankful for senses because I was ready either we both die or one of us goes.

snippets.context: [Onyango on the duration of his parents' violence]
snippets.text: Like those guys fought corner to corner from the time I was probably six, seven years old to the time I was 24, 25. Those were so many years of witnessing all these things. You know, and it was like sometimes if somebody is holding a knife, I've witnessed my mom's blood, too much of it, just too much blood. You know, and that was that was too much trauma for for me.

snippets.context: [Onyango on being the firstborn during the worst years]
snippets.text: I took a lot of those punches and it was just too tough. I was forced to grow up so fast, faster than my brain was ready for. And those things had very serious implications on my adult life, especially in my 20s and I didn't know how that would manifest.

snippets.context: [Onyango on running away to Nairobi streets at 16, sleeping on Koinange Street with sex workers]
snippets.text: When somewhere stops being conducive for your own dignity, you'd rather be disrespected by strangers. You choose to be disrespected by strangers than people who you know, people who you are supposed to trust. You'd take that in. So out there now I used to say I was shoplifting from supermarkets.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes the moment he refused his father's whip after being caught at Naivas supermarket]
snippets.text: It was the first day I stood up to my dad and I told him, 'You're not beating me.' You're not beating me. And true to it, it was the last day he beat me. You know, I think just being in the streets and seeing all everything I saw, I think it toughened me up a little. I was like, 'Fine, you have your demons, but you've given them to me also. Now they are mine. Now I can't I can't I can't take it anymore.'

snippets.context: [Onyango announces his mother has just moved into her own home — three weeks before this interview]
snippets.text: It will surprise you that my mother has just gotten the energy to live on her own this year. Literally three weeks ago as I say this. She's just moved into her own house. It's like she's starting life from scratch. And to me that is liberation. Every child wants to see their parents happy. Subconsciously. You want to see your caregivers.

snippets.context: [Onyango's claim about all sons]
snippets.text: Every son, every boy child wants to be like their father. They want to be close to their father. They want to be friends with their father.

snippets.context: [Onyango on the contradictory man his father became]
snippets.text: It was almost like he had two words inside him and something within him was fighting or something. But what changed also is the more money he started earning, the more violent he became at home. And those fights started becoming worse and worse and worse.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes the third runaway, when he wanted to die]
snippets.text: When I went to 29 years old, I actually got another bout of depression. And it came with suicidal thoughts. And in fact, at 16, that time I was 16, I also became suicidal. In fact, the third time I ran away from home now, I wanted to go kill myself. Somewhere nobody knew me. So I stole my dad's ATM. I had even used an ATM before. I wanted 500 shillings, not even much, just 500 to go to Mombasa and rot somewhere and die.

snippets.context: [Onyango on what shifted on the bus to Mombasa]
snippets.text: When I got to Mombasa, in fact, when I got to Mto wa Ndei, you know that heat starts hitting you. And you're like, do I really want to die? Am I sure I want to die? In essence, it wasn't that I really wanted to die. I just wanted the pain to stop.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes the moment he posted publicly about depression for the first time]
snippets.text: I went home, I took a shower for like two hours. That's why the water was just running, just running. And I I'm thinking, what do I do with myself when I get out of this place? I was living on the second floor of a building. Just as it was when I was 16 years old, words came to my rescue. So, I got out of the shower, wet as I was. I opened up my laptop. And I went to Facebook. And I wrote, 'I have depression. And I'm feeling suicidal today.' And that was it. That's also what changed everything.

snippets.context: [Onyango on the anger he felt about how he was educated]
snippets.text: I felt cheated. Cheated in the sense that I was 29 years old, and in all my life, all the education I've been through, nobody had taught me how my mind works. Nobody had taught me the importance of taking care of my mind. They taught me algebra, they taught me where Mandrongo not is. They taught me very simple things about the biology of a body and stuff, things like thorax, things like abdomen, things I've not even been using. But my mind, the thing that's keeping me alive, nobody has ever taught me about that.

snippets.context: [Onyango's neuroscience-informed framework for breaking inherited masculine violence]
snippets.text: Your brain is a simulation machine. It repeats patterns. Yeah. Our behaviors become behaviors because they are patterns of things that were done over and over that we picked up from. That's why you see children wearing their parents' shoes. They see their parents do this. We learn from what we see, right? And because I wanted a different life for myself, for my future, for the future communities I wanted to create, it meant I had to stop my brain from simulating the violence I was taught, the violence I was conditioned to, and create a new brain for myself. And that also feels like building another world because of the energy it takes.

snippets.context: [Onyango on how trauma shows up physically]
snippets.text: When you go through something traumatizing as a child, your brain could forget it. But your nervous system stores the memory. So that's why you hear somebody is taken to the hospital, maybe their body feels sickly. But there's no disease that is being found. Trauma responses manifest in very many different ways. Some people become anxious, some people overeat, some people overwork. Some people's bodies develop their immune systems go down, so they are prone to illnesses. Some people resort to drug abuse and substance. Some people are also very violent as a coping mechanism because they're trying to protect themselves. They don't want to be seen as weak.

snippets.context: [Onyango on how childhood damage hurt his adult relationships]
snippets.text: Now, because of those experiences as a child, you haven't seen what a healthy relationship looks like. I had it was very hard for me to keep healthy relationships as well. I struggled especially in my 20s because trusting was such a difficult thing. My dad being the person I should have trusted first, hurt me most.

snippets.context: [Onyango on the broader cultural silence around male emotional life]
snippets.text: In my 20s now you're meeting girls and your body is feeling excited. And nobody has ever taught you anything about sex. Even the church we go to. Do you know how far we are from the pastor up there? Very far. He's on the pulpit. You don't know anything about this guy's life. You don't even know if the pastor struggles. So, where is vulnerability? Nobody's really reaching out to you as a young person to say, you know what, you're actually not alone struggling with these emotions. Your parents don't talk to you about their problems. The pastor doesn't talk to you about what he's going through. People look so okay. They're like, they're all pretending they're fine.

snippets.context: [Onyango's commitment to public male disclosure]
snippets.text: Society is lying to me. Everybody is lying to me. And you know what, as a first step, I'll be the first man to start talking. So, I'll talk and I won't care whether I'll be judged because I'm a guy, I'm an African man. People look at you in certain ways because it's you, you want to be like a woman. It's only women who express themselves like that and stuff like that. But I'm like, if these things I'm doing will help me live longer, a happier, healthier life.

snippets.context: [Onyango uses geography as a metaphor for why men die younger]
snippets.text: It's not a mistake that women generally live longer than men. It's not a mistake. Outside how physically testing men's lives have been, it's also because we keep all that pain inside. It's not healthy. For example, even geography, why is a salty water lake salty? Because it only has an inlet, it doesn't have an outlet. A fresh water lake is fresh because it has both inlets and outlets. Your heart has veins that pump blood in and pump blood out. If it was only inlet, we wouldn't even survive. Our brains need air. People faint because air oxygen is out from the brain. You collapse. So essentially, space is basic to everything.

snippets.context: [Onyango on what he had to learn in adult relationships]
snippets.text: I struggled through my relationships, and even work because, you know, when you do not know how to express yourself, you wouldn't know how to say when you're unhappy. People will step on you. I didn't know how to keep boundaries because as a child my boundaries were overstepped. It took me so long to learn these things, to learn how to say, 'I need this. I need help.' To learn how to say, 'I need support. I am not happy with this. I'm not happy with that. Can I get assistance?' It took me so long to learn that.

snippets.context: [Onyango on the false confidence men inherit]
snippets.text: The way I was conditioned and very many boys are socialized, you grow up thinking men know everything because apparently dad is the one calling the shots at home. Men are the ones controlling resources everywhere you go. So, you actually think that a man asking for help, a man asking for directions is a bad thing to say.

snippets.context: [Onyango places his father's violence in historical context — his grandfather fought in WWII Burma for the British]
snippets.text: I went back and I wanted to understand his childhood from a genuinely curious place. And realizing he also grew up in a violent place. And his father was a polygamist. His father, my grandpa even fought for the second, in the Second World War in Burma. And a lot of those African men who fought for the British had serious post-traumatic stress that manifested in their lives differently. Some became completely redundant, they couldn't do anything. Some became struggling alcoholics. Some, their families just wear that away.

snippets.context: [Onyango's framework for how patriarchal homes collapse]
snippets.text: And then our homes being very patriarchal. If something is wrong with the man, the whole house goes down.

snippets.context: [Onyango on holding both compassion and accountability for his father]
snippets.text: It was funny because now I started looking at my dad as also a child who was in pain, you know? And it didn't excuse what he was doing, but I understood it. I understood, but I didn't excuse. What that gave me was a bit more emotional intelligence, because now I knew how to protect myself when he would act out.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes setting up a peer support community for people reaching out to him]
snippets.text: I set up a 200-member mental health support group online. It's just a WhatsApp group. I made a call on my socials and people reached out, like 200 people reached out. So, I opened two WhatsApp groups and I told them, 'Guys, look, nobody knows everything here. So, we are developing a community. A community that is like a village. All of us are here, all of us are important. All of us are creating content. Live as if you're living in a village, the way you'd want your village to run.'

snippets.context: [Onyango on his trauma-counseling clientele]
snippets.text: I support especially people who are battling with childhood traumas. And we are so many. You'd be surprised. We are so many from CEOs to fundi wa viatu. People battling with their relationship with their father, relationship with their mother, relationship with their siblings, relationship with their old tribe, like things people are holding in their hearts that they're struggling with. They don't have a language to express.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes his support group for male survivors of sexual abuse]
snippets.text: I have set up like a support, a safe space for men who have been sexually abused, which is another long story that I didn't share. One of the purposes I think I have in life is to support men to humanize themselves.

snippets.context: [Onyango analyzes how all powerful figures in society are men and yet emotionally inaccessible]
snippets.text: We've grown up in a world where your idea of God comes in the shape of a man. The idea of the head of the home comes in the shape of a man. The idea of a leader, all the big leaders you've seen probably in all your life globally have come in the shape of a man. But this same man, you can't talk to him. You can't see him. You can't see his emotions. This dad, this priest, this politician, this president, you can't reach him. He looks okay, he's rich, he looks wealthy, he looks all well put together, but you can't see his emotions.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes the limited emotional palette men are granted]
