snippets.text: He only expresses himself when he's extremely happy or extremely sad. When he's extremely sad, he's angry, he's violent. And he's extremely happy, he's with women and alcohol and doing all kinds of nasty stuff. So, in the subconscious of a young boy, you're living in the negative energy because you do not have communities of healthy men around you. They're not there.

snippets.context: [Onyango's mission statement for men]
snippets.text: I want to create life for these communities where men can rethink themselves. Men can re-see and start to imagine themselves differently. The things you are told about who a man is supposed to be, all of them were created. It was somebody else's idea. So, you can have your own idea of who a man is supposed to be or who you want to be as a man.

snippets.context: [Onyango's structural argument for why men's mental health matters to society]
snippets.text: When we sort out the issues men have with themselves, security will improve, governance will improve, relationships will improve, urban development will improve, nearly everything will improve.

snippets.context: [Onyango pushes back on the trend of demanding men cry as proof of vulnerability]
snippets.text: Now even the thing about crying. There are some quarters where men have been made to feel like if you don't cry, you're not doing it enough. You're not being vulnerable enough. People have different ways of expressing pain. As long as they know, they have that space to express pain in whatever manner is comfortable to them, that is what vulnerability is. And we cannot be vulnerable according to social standards if we have not learned how to be vulnerable to our own selves.

snippets.context: [Onyango contrasts how women organize collectively while men struggle to]
snippets.text: What are men doing? What are we doing as peers to say, let's come together and do something different. But we can't do that because individually we are struggling with things we don't talk about. So, we can't get together. Women struggle but they get together. That's how they are able to move. We struggle, we can't get together because we fear each other. 'Eh, how will Kivu see me? Eh, if I go to Brayo I will look weak, eh, so we should go drink and let this story end.'

snippets.context: [Onyango on the universality of male emotional suppression he sees from his BBC feature feedback]
snippets.text: I was recently featured on the BBC. Let me tell you, you know, like they translate these stories to different languages. And, man, men from South America, men from the deepest places of this earth reaching out to me saying, you know what? I do not think I have the courage to express myself like you do in my own community. This is not just an African man's thing. This is a global man's thing. Men are struggling all over the world to talk, just talking.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes some of the messages he receives from men]
snippets.text: I have like 19-year-olds reaching out to me saying, you know, I'm struggling with masturbation, I'm struggling with sex addiction, I'm struggling with substance abuse. I was raped when I was 19, I was raped when I was this. There's a story of a guy who reached out to me and he told me that he was sodomized by friends, people he knew. They sodomized him, robbed him, and he got so mad, so angry. He hired goons to go kill them. And he told me, he's sure they can't do such a thing to another person because they are rotting in their graves. Boys who are abused when they were kids. They're stuck. Pastors reach out to me. They stand on pulpits, my brother, but they're in so much pain.

snippets.context: [Onyango's redefined idea of what church/community should be]
snippets.text: Today, even when I think of church, it's not what I grew up knowing. When I think of church, I feel every home should be a church. Every home, every space, every space should be a safe space. Everywhere we go, we have to develop trauma-informed communities where we can humanize one another, where the first instinct when I see you and you're in trousers and a shirt, I'm not boxing you, 'Ah, you're a guy,' so I want to treat you like this because of the traumas other people other men have taken me through.

snippets.context: [Onyango on physical self-care as central to mental health]
snippets.text: Our bodies carry our emotions. Our bodies carry our truths. Our bodies carry our secrets. And all our love, all our pain. You have to take care of your body. It is the secret of health. Take care of your body. Check what you're eating, check who you're hanging around. Check the kind of work, how much rest you're getting. Check what you're feeding your mind. Take care of your body.

snippets.context: [Onyango introduces the neuroscience of social connection]
snippets.text: Communities are everything. We do not heal in isolation. There is something in science called neuroception. So neuroception is the ability of your nervous system to distinguish whether a person or a place or a situation is safe or unsafe for you. Now, why healthy relationships make us thrive is because our nervous systems are connecting. When our nervous systems connect in a healthy way, our bodies are like electricity. When we connect nicely, we thrive, we lighten up. So that's why when you stay in healthy community relationships and communities, people who've been living in those situations are happier, they're healthier, they also live longer. And your immune system also is very strong.

snippets.context: [Onyango refuses to give cheap motivational advice to suicidal men]
snippets.text: I don't like giving motivational quotes about, create some hope and keep keep on going. You're going to feel like giving up. This is real life. This is real life. You're going to go through things that will make you feel this is not worth it. And I usually just tap back to what I did. I don't tell people what they should do. I tell people what I did. I reached out to the last ounce of light that was left within me. That's what I did. Sometimes, because home was unbearable, I ran away from home. So I ran away from the trouble. It saved my life.

snippets.context: [Onyango describes the male friends who held him during depression]
snippets.text: I'm so lucky to have a group of male friends whom I've known for the last 10 years, who have held me together through these issues. So they, when I came out with my stories on depression, they didn't understand what depression was. Nobody in my circles knew what depression was. But what did my friends do? They would come to my house, male friends, eh? They would wash my house, they would cook for me, they would open my curtains. We have a DJ in there who's also a banker. So we would come to my house and he'd do live mixes in the house, just to liven me up. So, sometimes we are afraid we will be misunderstood because people won't understand. But people who love you don't have to understand everything. They just know you're not okay.

snippets.context: [Onyango's central principle as a trauma counselor]
snippets.text: There's a phrase in psychology we say, 'Trauma that is not transformed is transferred.' If you don't transform your pain into something else, you're going to transfer it. So that's what has been happening in many of our homes.

snippets.context: [Onyango's plea to parents]
snippets.text: What I would wish specifically because this is what created a lot of pain for me is that parents to just know their children are on their side. Your children are on your side. Your children don't need to see you as a perfect parent. Your children you don't need to be rich so that your children can see that you work so hard to take them to these places. No. Sometimes children just want to be held. They just want to be affirmed. You know what? You're a bright kid. You know what? Ah, you became number 16, number 17 out of 20. You can do better. Keep going. Not every time fingers, fingers, fingers, fingers. Every time kiboko, kiboko, kiboko. It's not working.

snippets.context: [Onyango on how violence cycles in boys' schools mirror generational violence]
snippets.text: It's the same thing when we were in high school in boys' schools. Umeingia form one, a form four is bullying you. When you get to form four, you do the same thing to a form one. It's the same thing. Those are vicious cycles of violence. We have to stop it. If we are to live in this world sustainably, we can't keep going on with all this mayhem. Our families are dying.

snippets.context: [Onyango opens his talk with the affectionate early memories of his father, including his father's gymnastic athleticism and refereeing matches]
snippets.text: When I was five years old, my daddy would pick me up and put me on the table in our living room, switch on his Sanyo radio and tell me to dance for him. Nearly every evening we had walks with dad when he'd hold my hand as we visited his friends' homes having tea in almost every house as they chit chatted about politics and life. He used to buy me Taifa Leo newspapers and set me on his lap, teach me how to read Swahili. I enjoyed watching him become the man he was. My dad was also a very flexible gymnast, and he used to be a school referee as well. And because we were both football fans, watching your dad officiating a match really felt like you conquered the world.

snippets.context: [Onyango on the lasting impact of the burning that night]
snippets.text: On top of that, I had felt like something had been broken inside me. Something to this date, I still don't have a language to express. I felt like I had been violated in a spiritual way, in a way that there's no words to put. And so, the world started feeling so unsafe for me, and I kept wondering, if that's something my father would do to me, how about the rest of the world that's full of strangers? What's gonna happen to me? I'm only 10. And that is about the time I began struggling with anxiety. Very quietly, but the world within me was just screaming all the time.

snippets.context: [Onyango on his form-one depression onset]
snippets.text: When I was 15, the pressure was catching up, and I did have my first bout with depression. I was a Form 1 student, and I remember a very particular time when the zeal to read just disappeared from me. I was dealing with abandonment issues, and the school we were in, there were so many violent things also happening, and so I had triggers almost in every corner of my life.

snippets.context: [Onyango on what was running through his mind in 2017 before the Facebook post]
snippets.text: I have lost two peers, friends of mine, one who died by global blood pressure, and another one who took away his life. Really young guys. I'm thinking to myself, man, why is life so hard? You know? I'm thinking of my friends, and I'm like, wherever club boss is, he's not thinking about fair, or rent, or an argument with his girlfriend. He's resting. Why can't I join him? And those suicidal thoughts came back to me, really, really strongly.

snippets.context: [Onyango on what therapy gave him]
snippets.text: I got into therapy, and through it, I discovered so many things about myself. And I came across so many words like depression, like anxiety, like post-traumatic stress, which were just so humongous things those years. But it was the fact that I could name my pain. That gave me hope. There is a way out.

snippets.context: [Onyango's closing manifesto]
snippets.text: All of you and all of me, we deserve joy. We deserve love. We deserve friendship. No matter how much the affliction you've been through as a person, as a community, as a family, if you are willing, if you want to get better, it's possible. I used to tell myself when I was younger, when I grow up, I want to find peace. If I don't find it, I will create it. Your trauma does not have to define you. I am defined by love. We are brilliant. We are beautiful. We are joyous people, but also we can be free.

snippets.text:
Yesterday, when I rode in an Über, I encountered a very spiritual moment.

The driver was a young man, jovial and radiating positivity, despite the slow economic environment affecting Über's business.

In the passenger's pouch was a Holy Bible, and I asked him why he was keeping it there. 

This is what he told me,

"A month ago, I buried my Father, and this scripture has been my father's voice.

When I wake up in the morning, I read a chapter, and when I return home to my wife and daughter, I read another chapter.

If an Über driver arrives home safely, it is by grace because what we encounter on these roads can be disheartening and heartbreaking.

The scripture has helped me heal from the loss of my Father because every time I read, the scripture echoes my father's voice."

I asked him if he is born-again, and he answered,

"I am not born-again, I am just in sync with my spirituality. 

I don't have to be born-again.

My father used to get disappointed when someone introduced themselves as "I am so and so, and I am born-again."

The people who tell us that they are born-again are actually using Christianity to commit sin and immorality.

Your behaviour, habits, words and actions are enough to prove that you are a righteous man living in accordance with spiritual sincerity.

You don't have to tell us because the tongue is deceitful, but the heart is sincere."

I was satisfied with his sentiments.

It was inspirational and memorable.

May all those looking for a spiritual anchor find peace in their lives.

Life is chaotic, unfulfilling, and filled with envy and corruption.

May your lives be inspired with joy and buoyancy.

May your prayers and dreams become fruitful.

Amen!

#BetterTogether

There is a trending conversation on Kenyan X about the deteriorating Public Health System.

Governors, Senators and MCAs influence the structure, resources and frameworks that govern the public health sector.

The photos you are seeing were taken by me back in 2019 when I was heading a public hospital.

This woman had been assaulted by her husband after an evening quarrel because of food.

When she came to the emergency department, her ear was dangling like a soot strand hanging precariously from my grandmother's hut.

In the emergency department, at the minor theatre, in the emergency tray:

• There were no stitching needles or surgical sutures.

• There were no sterilised packs.

• There was no lignocaine for local anaesthesia.

I had no choice but to fix her earlobe.

This was the time when NHIF revenue allocation had just been transferred from the Hospital Management Team to the County Treasury, where County Officials decided who, when, and how much hospitals would receive to fund their budgets.

So, because there were no supplies, I had to beg the maternity matron for them, promising to replenish them.

I did the work. I fixed the earlobe and discharged her.

She didn't have any money with her, which was, of course, obvious, given the family's disagreement over food.

So, I had to dip into my pocket to buy Lignocaine, sutures and Jik to sterilise the pack I had borrowed.

The public health system has been in a mess because self-serving politicians distorted the spirit of devolving the health function.

These politicians spend county resources on buildings, aesthetics, decor, colour, and public relations exercises, except for the basics like:

• Adequate supplies.

• A motivated health care staff.

• A reliable and accessible system.

Unless we overhaul the entire system of political brinkmanship and wheeler-dealers,

snippets.platform: YouTube (A Girl Dad on a Mission, Finale)
snippets.context: [Philip directly asking community members what they need]
snippets.text: How can we help you? What would you like to see happen in this village?

snippets.context: [Philip's caption launching the documentary, written for International Women's Day]
snippets.text: When I broke my leg last year, I thought the hardest part would be the pain and the stillness. But as I sat at home, watching the news, I was confronted by something far more painful, the endless stories of women suffering from violence and injustice.

snippets.context: [Philip's caption continued — articulating his motivation as a father]
snippets.text: As a father to a little girl, I couldn't look away. I couldn't pretend this wasn't the world she would grow up in. So, I decided to do something.

snippets.context: [Philip's caption continued — what the journey revealed]
snippets.text: I traveled across the country, meeting survivors, hearing their stories, and witnessing firsthand the brutal reality so many women face daily. What I saw changed me. It broke my heart, but it also opened my eyes.

snippets.context: [Philip's caption continued — articulating his identity as both filmmaker and father]
snippets.text: This documentary is my journey, not just as a storyteller, but as a father who wants a better world for his daughter.

snippets.context: [Philip's caption closing]
snippets.text: I hope it opens your eyes too. I hope it moves all of us to act. Because justice isn't just an idea, it's a fight we all must take part in.

snippets.context: [Philip reflecting — at Richard Njau's prompt — on how his family insulated him from Dandora's violence]
snippets.text: (In response to Richard noting that the insulation of love within Philip's family made it a safe space despite the violence outside.) Let me tell you if there's one thing my dad used to beat me up a lot about was me going out to the estate to play.

snippets.context: [Philip references his daughter while discussing how his Dandora exposure compares with hers]
snippets.text: I and you know I didn't realize it was not normal until I was in college. My daughter today is her birthday she's never seen a dead body.

snippets.platform: YouTube (A Girl Dad on a Mission, Ep. 2)
snippets.context: [Philip narrating the contrast between the coast's beauty and the violence happening there]
snippets.text: As I walked along these shores, I felt an urgent call for change. A desperate need for rapid economic growth and mass civic education. It raises a poignant question, Why does our government not see the urgency in addressing these issues? Here at the Kenyan coast, the beauty of the ocean starkly contrasts with the challenges faced by its people. It is a call to action, a plea for awareness and compassion.

comments.target_original_post: Original post asks why some men are satisfied with one woman; target claim concerns monogamy, sexual variety, and women's role in male satisfaction.
comments.comment: Studies in shared cultures show monogamous marriages tend to be more stable: lower divorce rates overall, with polygynous ones (esp. 3+ wives) up to 5x higher. Women in polygamous setups face 2.25x higher depression odds and worse family functioning/satisfaction per 2021 meta-analysis. Husbands often report higher satisfaction with extra wives. Recent data finds no clear child outcome edge for monogamy, sometimes polygyny aids resilience in tough conditions. Success hinges on culture and people.

While women fantasize about relationships, men fantasize about greatness. While women fantasize about cuddling, men fantasize about conquering. As men, we want to do something.
We crave significance, influence, and impact

A man's desire for greatness is so strong that he will attempt to experience it through someone else eg A man who shows up with a jersey with the number of a player always considered to be great.

GRAPHIC PHOTOS: There is a trending conversation on Kenyan X about the deteriorating Public Health System. This is a conversation that never ends. The public health system gets worse every year because we have allowed politicians to infiltrate the health system. Governors, Senators and MCAs influence the structure, resources and frameworks that govern the public health sector. They decide who receives supplies, who receives resources and what framework runs the system. All this is to siphon resources allocated to the delivery of public health services. They cripple our health sector, but when they fall sick, they fly abroad. The photos you are seeing were taken by me back in 2019 when I was heading a public hospital. This woman had been assaulted by her husband after an evening quarrel because of food. When she came to the emergency department, her ear was dangling like a soot strand hanging precariously from my grandmother's hut. In the emergency department, at the minor theatre, in the emergency tray: • There were no stitching needles or surgical sutures. • There were no sterilised packs. • There was no lignocaine for local anaesthesia. I had no choice but to fix her earlobe. This was the time when NHIF revenue allocation had just been transferred from the Hospital Management Team to the County Treasury, where County Officials decided who, when, and how much hospitals would receive to fund their budgets. So, because there were no supplies, I had to beg the maternity matron for them, promising to replenish them. I did the work. I fixed the earlobe and discharged her. She didn't have any money with her, which was, of course, obvious, given the family's disagreement over food. So, I had to dip into my pocket to buy Lignocaine, sutures and Jik to sterilise the pack I had borrowed. The public health system has been in a mess because self-serving politicians distorted the spirit of devolving the health function. These politicians spend county resources on buildings, aesthetics, decor, colour, and public relations exercises, except for the basics like: • Adequate supplies. • A motivated health care staff. • A reliable and accessible system. Unless we overhaul the entire system of political brinkmanship and wheeler-dealers, Unless we say enough is enough and commit ourselves to draining the swamp, Unless we stop glamorizing corruption, Unless we stop sterilizing negative ethno-chauvinist politicians, Unless we stop pretending to detest corruption, yet when it favours us, we say it is "God's Grace," — We shall continue gnashing our teeth, year in and year out. The people who are silent when doctors, clinicians, nurses and other healthcare staff are complaining about dwindling public health services are the same people who silently collude with corrupt politicians to supply substandard resources in our public hospitals. CHANGE or PERISH #BetterTogether

BOOTCAMP UPDATES, Day 4, 14th August 2025. The first lesson, facilitated by Jeff, focused on Adulthood and Manhood, Learners were introduced to essential tools for manhood, including having — A clear vision. — A mission. — A purpose. — The 5 Ms of Masculinity. Additional topics included: • Emotional intelligence • Respect and responsibility • Relationship building. • Time management. • Personal grooming. • Conflict resolution. The second session, led by Oscar Lumire, centered on Talents and Gifts, helping participants differentiate between innate abilities and learned skills and align them with their purpose. Using the Parable of the Talents, role-plays, and the My Gifts & Talents Action Planner, the learners explored strengths, challenges, and created actionable plans through the 5 W’s & H framework. In the evening, Eroll Siang'a conducted a practical lesson on FIRE SAFETY and FIREFIGHTING. The training covered the basics of fire: • Causes, and categories. • Fire prevention methods. • Firefighting equipment, including different types of extinguishers and their proper handling. Participants learned emergency response procedures and practiced the PASS method (Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep) in hands-on demonstrations. The day concluded with a fireside chat on self-actualization, aiming to help participants understand and pursue their highest potential. The conversation inspired the boys to evaluate their personal goals, improve self-awareness, and align their daily actions with long-term aspirations.
